title: The pure joy of puzzle solving—for a raccoon
html_body:
Today in Science and science: A reptile that learned to crawl before it learned to walk, exercise’s ripple effects, and more
Brought to you by From Concept to Reality: Taking Intelligent Agents into the Real World
View this email in your browser
10 March 2026
Today’s Visualized provides another answer to the age-old riddle, “What walks on four legs in the morning and two legs in the afternoon?” But first, catch up on the latest science news, including the joy of solving puzzles and the rippling benefits of exercise.
Animals  |  Animal Behaviour
The pure joy of puzzle solving—for a raccoon
Raccoons continue solving puzzles even when they’ve already gotten the only reward.  Hannah Griebling
People can spend hours trying to solve silly puzzles, like the one where you have to remove a dowel with a bolt through it from a glass bottle, even though there’s no reward other than knowing you bested a complex problem. Raccoons, it seems, feel a similar kind of glee at solving puzzles.

Researchers gave raccoons a puzzle box containing a single marshmallow. To get inside, they could choose from three entry points; some of these puzzle boxes were easily opened, like doors with no locks, while others were sealed shut with mechanisms much harder to crack. Intriguingly, the animals explored and opened other doors after they’d obtained the treat. “ We weren’t expecting them to open all three solutions in a single trial ,” said lead author Hannah Griebling in a statement. “They kept problem solving even when there was no marshmallow at the end.”

Their continued curiosity indicates intrinsic motivation, according to the researchers: They inherently enjoy gaining knowledge, perhaps because they receive a similar kind of mental reward to what humans feel solving puzzles. That could mean some of their annoying urban antics—like opening locked garbage cans—are done for fun, not just food. “Understanding the cognitive traits that help raccoons thrive can guide management of species that struggle, and inform strategies for other species, like bears, that use problem solving to access human-made resources,” said Griebling.
Read the paper
Health  |  Brain Communications
Exercise has ripple effects
Was exercising more one of your New Year’s resolutions? That’s good news for your brain because new research shows that bouts of physical movement can spark brain “ripples” beneficial for learning and memory.

Researchers already knew that the hippocampi in mice sent out high-frequency brain waves during physical activity. To see if the same held true in humans, a team recruited 14 epileptic participants to ride a stationary bike for 20 minutes. The researchers used a sensing technique called intracranial electroencephalography, which required the participants to have dozens of electrodes implanted, to record their neural activity before and after cycling.

The electrodes revealed that exercise increased the rate of the brain ripples, which connected the hippocampus with the cortical region, involved in memory and learning. A higher heart rate during exercise also correlated with more ripples when the movement ceased. Such rippling patterns matched well with those seen during noninvasive brain imaging on non-epileptic patients, which suggests that the ripples are a fundamental result of exercise.

“By directly recording brain activity, our study shows, for the first time in humans, that even a single bout of exercise can rapidly alter the neural rhythms and brain networks involved in memory and cognitive function,” author Michelle Voss said in a statement.
Read the paper
SPONSORED
Wallonia: A story of biopharma growth
What does biopharma growth look like on the ground in Wallonia, Belgium? This Q&A explores how the region has built research capacity, expanded lab space, and supported companies through public investment, clinical networks, and talent development.
Read more
Visualized
Artist's reconstruction of Sonselasuchus cedrus in its environment in what is now Petrified Forest National Park, 215 million years ago.  Artwork by Gabriel Ugueto 
 
Our young start on all fours, and must then learn how to walk. So, too, did the young of Sonselasuchus cedrus, a new study suggests.

S. cedrus was a crocodile relative that lived during the late Triassic, some 225 to 201 million years ago. Researchers found a trove of bones from these creatures, but were somewhat perplexed by their proportions. For smaller fossils, the front limbs are similar in size to the hind limbs. In larger fossils, they’re much smaller. “ The forelimb starts out like 75 per cent the length of the hind-limb, and then it ends up being more like 50 [per cent] ,” co-author Elliott Armour Smith told New Scientist. And the limbs’ bulk seemed to change over time, too. “The larger femurs in the population are rather robust,” said Armour Smith, while “even the largest humerus is relatively delicate.”

Why the disparity? Essentially, “We think these creatures started out their lives on four legs … they then started walking on two legs as they grew up,” Armour Smith explained in a statement.

The findings reveal that dinosaurs weren’t the only reptiles innovating evolutionarily. “When we see today’s crocodiles and alligators, we call them ‘living fossils,’” Armour Smith told ZME Science. But, he noted, “ these are animals that had a fantastic diversity of body shape, diets (herbivores and carnivores) and ecological domains (freshwater aquatic, terrestrial, and fully marine).”

As paleontologist Michelle Stocker told New Scientist, the lineage of reptiles that includes today’s crocodilians “[were] actually doing a lot of the really unique, crazy stuff first, and then dinosaurs are picking it up later.”
Read the paper
Et Cetera
E.T.’s calls dropped by space weather
The Search for Extraterrestrial Intelligence (SETI) Institute has been listening to the stars for decades, hoping to hear the distinct, focused sound of an alien civilization. But when they listened to radio transmissions from our own spacecraft, they discovered that space weather—like the solar wind that causes auroras here on Earth—blurs signals, enough to knock them out of the window where SETI has been listening. Still, “I don’t think they’ve wasted their time,” said one expert. “They have been trying things, and they’ve got pretty strong evidence that what they’re trying doesn’t work, because they haven’t found anything yet.”
The Astrophysical Journal Paper  |  Read more at New Scientist
A vitamin a day keeps the methylation at bay
Taking a daily multivitamin reduced the biological signs of aging—as measured by DNA methylation markers—in a group of 958 people with an average age of 70. While only two of the five markers studied showed this slowing of aging, “this kind of consistency across different epigenetic clocks is exactly what you want to see,” said one expert. Besides, the goal is “not just identifying how to live longer, but also how to live better,” said one of the study’s authors, and “the multivitamin intervention appeared to be on that type of trajectory over two years.”
Nature Medicine Paper  |  Read more at Nature
Smart slime
The slime mold Physarum polycephalum shocked scientists 16 years ago when it almost exactly produced the efficient connections between cities surrounding Tokyo that people spent years figuring out. It continues surprising researchers with its apparent intellect—even though it lacks any semblance of a brain.
Read more at Knowable
Without asserting sovereignty over their critical mineral resources, Global Majority countries will remain locked in an exploitative relationship to AI development, providing the materials and labor that power the technology while frontier AI powers capture its economic rewards and governance authority.
Expert Voices  |  5 March 2026  |  Chinasa T. Okolo
Last but not least
I am just so excited that someone actually looked into the #singingdogs phenomenon—and even more excited that they found evidence that some pups coordinate their pitches with music. Such. Good. Dogs.
Christie Wilcox, Editor, ScienceAdviser

With contributions from Hannah Richter
